MATH 3190 Homework 7

Due March 26, 2026
Focus: Notes 8
Total Points: 90 + 10 (Self-Critique Comment and Uploading to GitHub) = 100

Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_7 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.

Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (57 points)

In Homework 6, we briefly looked at a small sample from this data set. Now we have the full data set with 20,640 districts. For districts in California from 1990, we want to predict the median house value for the district based on the district location (longitude and latitude), the median house age in the block group, the total number of rooms in the homes, the total number of bedrooms, the population in the district, the number of households, and the median income (in $10,000). These data were obtained from scikit-learn.org and can be found in the Data folder of the Math3190_Sp26 GitHub repo with the name cal_housing.data.

library(math3190package)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

Part a (5 points)

Read in the data. The read.csv() function works for files that end in .data if they are comma separated like this file is. However, be aware that the housing data does not have column names, so make sure to put header = FALSE. Assign appropriate column names to the columns and then take logs (base e) of all variables except lat and long. Once this is done, split the data into training and testing sets with the testing containing 20% of the values. Set a seed when you do this.

# Read it in and adjust
column_names <- c(
  "longitude",
  "latitude",
  "housingMedianAge",
  "totalRooms",
  "totalBedrooms",
  "population",
  "households",
  "medianIncome",
  "medianHouseValue"
)

housing <- read.csv('cal_housing.data', header = FALSE, col.names = column_names) |>
  mutate(across(-c(longitude, latitude), log))

# Split
set.seed(2026)
sample_size <- floor(0.80 * nrow(housing))
train_indices <- sample(seq_len(nrow(housing)), size = sample_size)
train_set <- housing[train_indices, ]
test_set <- housing[-train_indices, ]

# Verify
cat("Training set size:", nrow(train_set), "\n")
Training set size: 16512 
cat("Testing set size: ", nrow(test_set), "\n")
Testing set size:  4128

Part b (5 points)

Use the following command to view pair-wise scatter plots for the data here. Change housing_train to whatever you called the training set and be sure to change the eval option for the code chunk to true. Note, that lower option changes the points to be plotted with periods instead of circles. This speeds up plotting time considerably and makes the plots more readable since there are many data points here.

Based on these plots, does multicollinearity appear to be an issue here? Explain.

library(GGally)
ggpairs(train_set, lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", shape = ".")))
  • A lot of the variables have high correlation coefficients with each other. Some of the scatterplots look like straight lines.

Part c (4 points)

Fit a OLS regression model predicting house prices from all other variables. Check its summary output and its VIF values. Comment on the VIFs.

# OLS
housing_lm <- lm(medianHouseValue ~ ., train_set)
summary(housing_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = medianHouseValue ~ ., data = train_set)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-2.7695 -0.1912 -0.0035  0.1847  2.6605 

Coefficients:
                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)      -9.608554   0.342034 -28.092  < 2e-16 ***
longitude        -0.255862   0.003869 -66.139  < 2e-16 ***
latitude         -0.262652   0.003732 -70.383  < 2e-16 ***
housingMedianAge  0.066616   0.004917  13.548  < 2e-16 ***
totalRooms       -0.302866   0.016792 -18.036  < 2e-16 ***
totalBedrooms     0.543872   0.022026  24.693  < 2e-16 ***
population       -0.389407   0.009768 -39.866  < 2e-16 ***
households        0.162506   0.020156   8.062 7.99e-16 ***
medianIncome      0.825597   0.008913  92.632  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.3256 on 16503 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6757,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6756 
F-statistic:  4299 on 8 and 16503 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
#VIFs
library(car)
vif(housing_lm)
       longitude         latitude housingMedianAge       totalRooms 
        9.352446         9.879043         1.212332        24.468716 
   totalBedrooms       population       households     medianIncome 
       40.100235         8.015435        33.654225         2.768977
  • The only value not close to 10 or way above it is medianIncome. Lots of multicollinearity.

Part d (3 points)

Fit a principal component model using the pcr() function in the pls package. Since the variables are on very different scales, use the scale = TRUE option in the function. Then find the VIF values for this model. The vif() function from the car library won’t work here. Instead, you can take the diagonals of the inverse of the correlation matrix (like is shown in the regularization section of Notes 7) for the scores output. The code for this is diag(solve(cor(pca_model$scores))). Of course, you need to change pca_model to what you called your model.

library(pls)
housing_pca <- pcr(medianHouseValue ~ ., data = train_set, scale = TRUE, validation = "CV")
pcr_vifs <- diag(solve(cor(housing_pca$scores)))
print(pcr_vifs)
Comp 1 Comp 2 Comp 3 Comp 4 Comp 5 Comp 6 Comp 7 Comp 8 
     1      1      1      1      1      1      1      1

Part e (5 points)

Now take the summary of your principal component model. With PCA, the common amount of variation we want to explain in the predictors is 90% or more. How many components are needed to achieve this? For that number of components, how much of the variation in log of home values is explained? How many components are needed to explain a “good” amount of the variation in the log of home values? Note: the upper bound on the amount of variation in the log of home values here with all 8 components will be equal to the \(R^2\) value of the OLS model.

summary(housing_pca)
Data:   X dimension: 16512 8 
    Y dimension: 16512 1
Fit method: svdpc
Number of components considered: 8

VALIDATION: RMSEP
Cross-validated using 10 random segments.
       (Intercept)  1 comps  2 comps  3 comps  4 comps  5 comps  6 comps
CV          0.5717   0.5667   0.5649   0.4897   0.4037   0.4028   0.3276
adjCV       0.5717   0.5667   0.5649   0.4897   0.4037   0.4027   0.3276
       7 comps  8 comps
CV      0.3277   0.3259
adjCV   0.3277   0.3259

TRAINING: % variance explained
                  1 comps  2 comps  3 comps  4 comps  5 comps  6 comps  7 comps
X                  48.799   72.624    86.40    96.94    98.60    99.47    99.82
medianHouseValue    1.773    2.396    26.66    50.16    50.41    67.20    67.21
                  8 comps
X                  100.00
medianHouseValue    67.57
  • It takes 4 components to explain 90% of the data
  • 4 components explains 50.16% of the variablity in log median house value
  • This model caps out at an R^2 value of about 0.6757 which is so-so. 6 PCs gets us to 0.6720.

Part f (4 points)

Now fit a partial least squares model using the plsr() function in the pls package with scale = TRUE. Then find the VIF values for this model like you did in part (d) and comment on them.

pls_model <- plsr(medianHouseValue ~ ., data = train_set, scale = TRUE, validation = "CV")
pls_vifs <- diag(solve(cor(pls_model$scores)))
print(pls_vifs)
Comp 1 Comp 2 Comp 3 Comp 4 Comp 5 Comp 6 Comp 7 Comp 8 
     1      1      1      1      1      1      1      1
  • No multicolinearity. All VIF values are 1

Part g (4 points)

Now take the summary of your partial least squares model. Compare this output to the summary of the PCA model. Explain what the differences are.

summary(pls_model)
Data:   X dimension: 16512 8 
    Y dimension: 16512 1
Fit method: kernelpls
Number of components considered: 8

VALIDATION: RMSEP
Cross-validated using 10 random segments.
       (Intercept)  1 comps  2 comps  3 comps  4 comps  5 comps  6 comps
CV          0.5717    0.438   0.3941   0.3811   0.3577   0.3274   0.3267
adjCV       0.5717    0.438   0.3941   0.3811   0.3577   0.3273   0.3267
       7 comps  8 comps
CV      0.3259   0.3259
adjCV   0.3259   0.3259

TRAINING: % variance explained
                  1 comps  2 comps  3 comps  4 comps  5 comps  6 comps  7 comps
X                   25.87    61.16    78.15    91.38    97.85    99.43    99.66
medianHouseValue    41.34    52.51    55.60    60.91    67.28    67.41    67.57
                  8 comps
X                  100.00
medianHouseValue    67.57
  • It takes more components to explain the X, but explains the y variable much quicker

How many components are needed to balance a good amount of variation explained in \(X\) and in \(y\) for the PLS model?

  • 5 components explain 97.85% of X and 67.28% of y so I think it is the sweet spot.

Part h (8 points)

In part (e), you said how many components were needed for the PCA to explain a good amount of the \(Y\) variable. Using that number of components, use the PCA and PLS models to find the root MSE when predicting the testing set. Note: the root MSE (or residual standard error, also abbreviated RMSE) is found by taking the square root of the sum of squared residuals divided by the residual degrees of freedom, which is \(n-p-1\), where \(p\) is the number of predictors in the model. Also, be aware that when you use the predict() function on your model, it will give you predictions for each number of components. You can access the predictions for using 3 components, for example, by typing predict(pca_model, test_set)[,,3].

Then in part (g), you said how many components were needed for the PLS Using that number of components. Using this number of components, use the PCA and PLS models to find the root MSE predicting the testing set.

# Define parameters from previous parts
p_e <- 6
p_g <- 5
n <- nrow(test_set)

# RMSE
RMSE <- function(y, y_hat, size, components) {
  sqrt(sum((y - y_hat)^2) / (size - components - 1))
}

# 6 Components (e)
pred_pca_6 <- predict(housing_pca, test_set)[,, p_e]
pred_pls_6 <- predict(pls_model, test_set)[,, p_e]

rmse_pca_6 <- RMSE(test_set$medianHouseValue, pred_pca_6, n, p_e)
rmse_pls_6 <- RMSE(test_set$medianHouseValue, pred_pls_6, n, p_e)

# --- 5 Components (g)
pred_pca_5 <- predict(housing_pca, test_set)[,, p_g]
pred_pls_5 <- predict(pls_model, test_set)[,, p_g]

rmse_pca_5 <- RMSE(test_set$medianHouseValue, pred_pca_5, n, p_g)
rmse_pls_5 <- RMSE(test_set$medianHouseValue, pred_pls_5, n, p_g)

# --- Final Output ---
cat(
  "Results using 6 components (Part E):\n",
  "- PCA RMSE:",
  rmse_pca_6,
  "\n",
  "- PLS RMSE:",
  rmse_pls_6,
  "\n\n"
)
Results using 6 components (Part E):
 - PCA RMSE: 0.3295653 
 - PLS RMSE: 0.3286117 
cat(
  "Results using 5 components (Part G):\n",
  "- PCA RMSE:",
  rmse_pca_5,
  "\n",
  "- PLS RMSE:",
  rmse_pls_5,
  "\n"
)
Results using 5 components (Part G):
 - PCA RMSE: 0.409319 
 - PLS RMSE: 0.3296511

Compare the results of the predictions in both cases.

  • Both models have a fairly comperable RMSE with a value of 0.3296 for PCA and 0.3286 for PLS using 6 components. At 5 components the PLS’s RMSE stays appoximately the same, but the PCA model jumps up to 0.4093.

Part i (12 points)

Using the number of components you said were needed for the PLS model in part (g), come up with reasonable surrogates for each of those components. Look at the projection output for this. When creating your surrogates, make sure to center and scale each variable. You can do this with the scale() function and putting your housing data frame or tibble in it.

#|label: 1i_surrogate

print(pls_model$projection[, 1:5])
                      Comp 1     Comp 2      Comp 3     Comp 4      Comp 5
longitude        -0.02714932 -0.2830744 -0.74347422 -0.6061546 -1.21451408
latitude         -0.26288931 -0.1946899 -0.23815805 -1.0798198 -1.02840464
housingMedianAge  0.05962689  0.3549460  0.46403794  0.1800045 -0.58677044
totalRooms        0.25072840 -0.2475005 -0.09169446 -0.3574486 -0.47121179
totalBedrooms     0.11782225 -0.3219127  0.32310321  0.4508212  0.58512568
population        0.03414942 -0.4962525 -0.23851637 -0.5417831 -0.81601539
households        0.15175031 -0.2876353  0.31823274  0.4078554  0.48085092
medianIncome      0.90865593  0.8904469  0.01856615 -0.1251027 -0.02409849
housing_scaled <- scale(train_set |> select(-medianHouseValue)) |>
  as.data.frame()

surrogates <- housing_scaled |>
  transmute(
    medianHouseValue = train_set$medianHouseValue,
    S1 = medianIncome,
    S2 = population,
    S3 = housingMedianAge,
    S4 = (latitude - longitude) / 2,
    S5 = totalRooms / households
  )

cor(surrogates)
                 medianHouseValue          S1           S2          S3
medianHouseValue       1.00000000  0.67932630  0.025530677  0.04457806
S1                     0.67932630  1.00000000  0.046192003 -0.15233041
S2                     0.02553068  0.04619200  1.000000000 -0.22166175
S3                     0.04457806 -0.15233041 -0.221661749  1.00000000
S4                    -0.08984331 -0.03592365 -0.126687749  0.05242873
S5                    -0.02532998 -0.02875306 -0.002805731  0.01354094
                           S4           S5
medianHouseValue -0.089843305 -0.025329984
S1               -0.035923647 -0.028753061
S2               -0.126687749 -0.002805731
S3                0.052428732  0.013540938
S4                1.000000000 -0.008138986
S5               -0.008138986  1.000000000

Then fit a OLS model using those surrogates in the training set. Check the VIF of this model to make sure each one is below 5. If they are not, your surrogates should be changed. You can use the vif() function in the car package here.

surrogate_lm <- lm(medianHouseValue ~ ., data = surrogates)

summary(surrogate_lm)

Call:
lm(formula = medianHouseValue ~ ., data = surrogates)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-2.76712 -0.26277 -0.00641  0.24571  2.66148 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error  t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  1.208e+01  3.180e-03 3799.784  < 2e-16 ***
S1           4.002e-01  3.219e-03  124.303  < 2e-16 ***
S2           1.118e-02  3.283e-03    3.404 0.000666 ***
S3           9.110e-02  3.296e-03   27.637  < 2e-16 ***
S4          -4.118e-02  3.270e-03  -12.592  < 2e-16 ***
S5          -1.245e-04  8.791e-05   -1.417 0.156543    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.4085 on 16506 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.4896,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4895 
F-statistic:  3167 on 5 and 16506 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
library(car)
vif(surrogate_lm)
      S1       S2       S3       S4       S5 
1.025434 1.066664 1.075105 1.017791 1.001007

Part j (7 points)

Now using that model you fit in the previous part with the surrogates, find the root MSE for predicting the testing set using that model, and compare it to what you got in part (h). Describe some pros and cons of the surrogate model.

Hint: to predict using this model, you will have to create a newdata data frame (or tibble) and then redefine your surrogates in that data frame using the scaled testing data set. Make sure the variable names in the newdata data frame match the variables names used in the model you defined in part (i).

# Update test set
test_scaled <- as.data.frame(scale(test_set |> select(-medianHouseValue)))

test_surrogates <- test_scaled |>
  transmute(
    S1 = medianIncome,
    S2 = population,
    S3 = housingMedianAge,
    S4 = (latitude - longitude) / 2,
    S5 = totalRooms / households
  )

# Predict
pred_surrogate <- predict(surrogate_lm, newdata = test_surrogates)

#RMSE
p_surr <- 5
n_test <- nrow(test_set)
rmse_surrogate <- sqrt(
  sum((test_set$medianHouseValue - pred_surrogate)^2) / (n_test - p_surr - 1)
)

# Compare
cat(
  "RMSE Comparison:\n",
  "- PCA (6 comps):",
  rmse_pca_6,
  "\n",
  "- PLS (5 comps):",
  rmse_pls_5,
  "\n",
  "- Surrogate OLS:",
  rmse_surrogate,
  "\n"
)
RMSE Comparison:
 - PCA (6 comps): 0.3295653 
 - PLS (5 comps): 0.3296511 
 - Surrogate OLS: 0.4150309
  • The surrogates have the highest RMSE out of all of the models. It will also be the least accurate model. It is however much more interperitable than the others and most likely more less colinear than the PLS model.

Problem 2 (33 points)

In base R, there is a dataset called penguins. Type ?penguins to read a little about the data.

Part a (6 points)

Remove the rows in the penguins dataset that have missing values. Then use the ggpairs() function from GGally to make pairwise scatterplots for the four quantitative variables and color the points based on the species variable. Note that the ggpairs() function has a columns argument where you can select the columns you want to plot and it has a mapping argument where you can put aes(color = species, alpha = 0.5) to color the points by species.

Include the plot here and then comment on which scatterplots seem to nicely separate the penguins by species and which tend to have worse separation.

# penguins was not base R
library(palmerpenguins)
library(GGally)
library(tidyverse)

penguins_clean <- penguins |>
  drop_na()

# pairwise scatterplots
ggpairs(
  penguins_clean,
  columns = 3:6,
  mapping = aes(color = species, alpha = 0.5),
  lower = list(continuous = wrap("points", size = 0.5))
)
  • Anything crossed with bill length seems to seperate nicely.
  • All other scatterplot mingle the Chinstrap and Gentoo species

Part b (4 points)

Run a scaled principal component analysis on the four quantitative variables using the prcomp() function. How much of the total variation in these variables is explained by the first two principal components? You can use the summary() function on your PCA to find this easily.

penguins_pca <- prcomp(penguins_clean[, 3:6], scale = TRUE)
summary(penguins_pca)
Importance of components:
                          PC1    PC2     PC3     PC4
Standard deviation     1.6569 0.8821 0.60716 0.32846
Proportion of Variance 0.6863 0.1945 0.09216 0.02697
Cumulative Proportion  0.6863 0.8809 0.97303 1.00000
  • 88% of the variation is explained by the fist 2 components

Part c (6 points)

Plot the first two principal components with PC1 on the x-axis and PC2 on the y-axis. Color the points by species and comment on how well the first two principal components are separating by species.

pca_scores <- as_tibble(penguins_pca$x) |> 
  mutate("species" = penguins_clean$species)

ggplot(pca_scores, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Penguin PCA: PC1 vs PC2",
    subtitle = "Colored by Species",
    x = "PC1",
    y = "PC2"
  )
  • Gentoo is pretty well seperated, but there is some mingling with the other 2

Part d (12 points)

Now use the umap() function in the umap library to fit a UMAP to the scaled quantitative variables in the penguins dataset. You can use the scale() function to scale the variables. Make sure the UMAP gives two components and set a random_state in the function so your results are reproducible. Then plot the UMAP using the the umap_plot() function in math3190package and color the points by species using the color_umap argument. Experiment with at least three different number of neighbors (n_neighbors) and at least three different minimum distances (min_dist).

library(umap)
library(math3190package)
library(patchwork)

penguins_scaled <- scale(penguins_clean[, 3:6])

neighbors_test <- c(5, 15, 50)
dist_test <- c(0.01, 0.1, 0.5)

plot_list <- list()

# 3 x 3 matrix
for (n in neighbors_test) {
  for (d in dist_test) {
    temp_umap <- umap(
      penguins_scaled,
      n_neighbors = n,
      min_dist = d,
      n_components = 2,
      random_state = 2026
    )

    # Plot
    p <- umap_plot(temp_umap, data = penguins_clean, color_umap = "species") +
      labs(title = paste0("n=", n, ", dist=", d)) +
      theme(legend.position = "none") 

    plot_list[[length(plot_list) + 1]] <- p
  }
}

# 3x3 grid
(plot_list[[1]] | plot_list[[2]] | plot_list[[3]]) /
  (plot_list[[4]] | plot_list[[5]] | plot_list[[6]]) /
  (plot_list[[7]] | plot_list[[8]] | plot_list[[9]]) +
  plot_annotation(title = "Penguin UMAP Parameter Exploration (3x3 Grid)")

Part e (5 points)

Comment on the UMAP plots you made in part (d). Include a comparison to the PCA plot regarding how well these species are separated.

  • The Gentoo species is good and seperated. The other 2 are mostly seperated. It did a better job than the PCA plot at least.

Feel free to repeat this problem using a variable other than species! Sometimes the UMAP outperforms PCA at separating classes, sometimes it does a little worse, and sometimes there are both quite bad.

  • UMAPs can be quite confusing.
